label: Mongol Empire
pitch:
In a single generation, a nomadic herder named Temujin united the warring tribes of the Mongolian steppe, and at a great assembly in 1206 was Genghis Khan proclaimed universal ruler. Within 25 years, Mongol horsemen had conquered more territory than Rome managed in four centuries — from the Siege of Baghdad that ended the Abbasid Caliphate to Kublai Khan founding the Yuan dynasty in China. The empire wasn't just destruction; it was the Reopening of the Silk Road on a scale the world had never seen.

🔑 Key Facts:
• The Great Yasa established a legal code governing military and civil life, while the Decree of religious tolerance exempted clergy of all faiths from taxation
• The Siege of Baghdad in 1258 destroyed the Islamic world's intellectual capital, killing the last Abbasid caliph
• Marco Polo arrives in China and spent 17 years at Kublai Khan's court, later dictating the travelogue that inspired Columbus
• The Secret History of the Mongols is the oldest surviving Mongolian literary work and the primary source for Genghis Khan's life

🤔 Why It Matters: Mongol conquests forged connections — the Yam relay postal system, Chao paper currency, and trade routes — that linked East and West, while Black Death spreads via Mongol routes carried plague that reshaped European demographics forever.

🎲 Wild Card: The Second invasion of Japan failed spectacularly when Kublai Khan's massive fleet was destroyed by a typhoon the Japanese called kamikaze ("divine wind") — a term that would take on very different meaning 700 years later.

spans.label: United Mongol Empire
spans.description: Under Genghis Khan and his immediate successors, a single Mongol state conquered more territory than Rome managed in four centuries. The Great Yasa legal code, religious tolerance, and the Yam postal system unified the vast empire.

spans.label: Golden Horde
spans.description: The western Mongol successor state ruled the Eurasian steppe from Russia to Central Asia under Batu Khan's descendants. The Horde collected tribute from Russian principalities for over two centuries and converted to Islam under Berke Khan.

spans.label: Chagatai Khanate
spans.description: The Central Asian successor state controlled the Silk Road heartland from Transoxiana to the Tarim Basin. It gradually split into eastern and western halves, with the western portion eventually giving rise to Timur's empire.

spans.label: Ilkhanate
spans.description: Founded by Hulegu Khan after the sack of Baghdad, the Ilkhanate ruled Persia, Iraq, and parts of Central Asia. Under Ghazan Khan it converted to Islam, and the court patronized groundbreaking work in astronomy and historiography.

spans.label: Yuan Dynasty
spans.description: Kublai Khan proclaimed a Chinese-style dynasty ruling all of China from the new capital at Khanbaliq (Beijing). The Yuan introduced paper currency, patronized drama and astronomy, and hosted Marco Polo before falling to the Ming rebellion.

events.label: Birth of Temüjin
events.description: Temüjin, the future Genghis Khan, was born near the Onon River in the Khentii Mountains of Mongolia.

events.label: Adoption of Uyghur script
events.description: After defeating the Naimans, the Mongols adopted the Uyghur script as their official writing system.

events.label: Genghis Khan proclaimed
events.description: Temüjin united the Mongol tribes and was proclaimed Genghis Khan (universal ruler) at a kurultai on the Onon River.

events.label: The Great Yasa
events.description: Genghis Khan promulgated the Great Yasa, a comprehensive legal code governing Mongol military, civil, and commercial life.

events.label: Decree of religious tolerance
events.description: Genghis Khan exempted clergy of all faiths from taxation and military service, establishing a policy of religious tolerance throughout the empire.

events.label: Reopening of the Silk Road
events.description: Mongol unification of the steppe secured caravan routes, revitalizing long-distance trade across Central Asia.

events.label: Invasion of Jin dynasty
events.description: Genghis Khan invaded the Jurchen Jin dynasty of northern China, beginning a campaign that would last over two decades.

events.label: Subutai's campaigns
events.description: Subutai, history's most successful general, directed over 20 campaigns conquering 32 nations across a wider area than any other commander.

events.label: Invasion of Khwarezmia
events.description: Genghis Khan invaded the Khwarezmian Empire after the governor of Otrar massacred a Mongol trade caravan.

events.label: Karakorum established
events.description: Genghis Khan established Karakorum in central Mongolia as the administrative capital of the expanding Mongol Empire.

events.label: Battle of the Kalka River
events.description: Jebe and Subutai defeated a combined Rus' and Cuman army at the Kalka River, the Mongols' first encounter with European forces.

events.label: Death of Genghis Khan
events.description: Genghis Khan died during the final campaign against Western Xia. His empire was divided among his four sons.

events.label: Ögedei Khan
events.description: Ögedei, Genghis Khan's third son, was elected Great Khan at a kurultai and oversaw massive imperial expansion.

events.label: Fall of the Jin dynasty
events.description: The Mongols and their Song allies captured Caizhou, ending the Jurchen Jin dynasty and securing all of northern China.

events.label: Yam relay postal system
events.description: Ögedei formalized the Yam, a vast relay postal system with stations every 25-30 miles across the empire.

events.label: Mongol invasion of Europe
events.description: Batu Khan and Subutai led the western campaign, devastating Rus' principalities, Poland, and Hungary.

events.label: The Secret History of the Mongols
events.description: The Secret History of the Mongols was composed, the oldest surviving Mongolian literary work and primary source for Genghis Khan's life.

events.label: Gunpowder spreads westward
events.description: Mongol campaigns introduced Chinese gunpowder weapons to the Middle East and Europe, transforming warfare across Eurasia.

events.label: Möngke Khan
events.description: Möngke became Great Khan after a contested succession, restoring central authority and launching campaigns against Song China and the Middle East.

events.label: William of Rubruck's journey
events.description: Franciscan friar William of Rubruck traveled to Karakorum as an envoy of Louis IX, leaving one of the best European accounts of the Mongol court.

events.label: Hülegü Khan founds Ilkhanate
events.description: Hülegü Khan established the Ilkhanate, ruling Persia, Iraq, and parts of Central Asia as a Mongol successor state.

events.label: Siege of Baghdad
events.description: Hülegü Khan sacked Baghdad, killing Caliph al-Musta'sim and ending the 500-year Abbasid Caliphate.

events.label: Maragheh Observatory
events.description: Hülegü Khan founded the Maragheh Observatory in Persia, where Nasir al-Din al-Tusi led groundbreaking astronomical research.

events.label: Battle of Ain Jalut
events.description: The Egyptian Mamluks defeated a Mongol army at Ain Jalut in Palestine, marking the first major Mongol defeat and halting their westward advance.

events.label: Kublai Khan
events.description: Kublai Khan became Great Khan and later founded the Yuan dynasty, ruling the largest contiguous land empire in history.

events.label: Chao paper currency
events.description: Kublai Khan issued the Chao, a government-backed paper currency that became the sole legal tender throughout the Yuan empire.

events.label: Toluid Civil War
events.description: Kublai Khan and his brother Ariq Böke fought a civil war over the succession, permanently fracturing the united empire.

events.label: Khanbaliq (Dadu) built
events.description: Kublai Khan began construction of Khanbaliq (modern Beijing) as the new capital of his empire, designed in a grand Chinese-Mongol style.

events.label: 'Phags-pa script created
events.description: Tibetan lama Drogön Chögyal Phagpa created the 'Phags-pa script at Kublai Khan's request as a unified writing system for the empire.

events.label: Tibetan Buddhism adopted by Yuan
events.description: Kublai Khan declared Tibetan Buddhism the state religion of the Yuan dynasty, with Phagpa as Imperial Preceptor.

events.label: Yuan dynasty proclaimed
events.description: Kublai Khan formally established the Yuan dynasty, adopting a Chinese dynastic name and blending Mongol and Chinese governance.

events.label: Marco Polo arrives in China
events.description: Venetian merchant Marco Polo arrived at Kublai Khan's court and spent 17 years in the empire, later dictating his famous travelogue.

events.label: Fall of the Song dynasty
events.description: The naval Battle of Yamen destroyed the last Song loyalists, completing the Mongol conquest of all China.

events.label: Guo Shoujing's Shoushi calendar
events.description: Yuan astronomer Guo Shoujing calculated the length of the year to within 26 seconds of the modern value, producing the Shoushi calendar.

events.label: Second invasion of Japan
events.description: Kublai Khan's massive fleet was destroyed by a typhoon (kamikaze) while attempting to invade Japan, ending Mongol ambitions there.

events.label: Ghazan converts to Islam
events.description: Ilkhan Ghazan converted to Islam, making it the official religion of the Ilkhanate and accelerating Persian-Mongol cultural fusion.

events.label: Sultaniyya built
events.description: Ilkhan Öljaitü built the city of Sultaniyya in Persia, featuring the massive Mausoleum of Öljaitü with its pioneering double-shell dome.

events.label: Rashid al-Din's Jami' al-Tawarikh
events.description: Ilkhanate vizier Rashid al-Din completed the Jami' al-Tawarikh (Compendium of Chronicles), the first true world history.

events.label: Black Death spreads via Mongol routes
events.description: The Black Death traveled along Mongol trade networks from Central Asia to Crimea, then to Europe, killing a third of the continent's population.

events.label: Fall of the Yuan dynasty
events.description: The Red Turban Rebellion drove the Mongols from China as Zhu Yuanzhang founded the Ming dynasty, ending Mongol rule in China.

events.label: Timur rises to power
events.description: Timur, claiming descent from Genghis Khan, established the Timurid Empire from Samarkand, conquering much of Central Asia and Persia.

events.label: Jebe — Mongol General
events.description: One of Genghis Khan's most trusted generals who conquered the Kara-Khitai and co-led the legendary reconnaissance expedition around the Caspian Sea.

events.label: Destruction of Samarkand
events.description: Genghis Khan sacked Samarkand, one of Central Asia's greatest cities, massacring defenders and deporting craftsmen to Mongolia.

events.label: Battle of Mohi
events.description: Subutai and Batu Khan destroyed the Hungarian army at Mohi, leaving Central Europe defenseless. Only Ogedei's death halted the advance.

events.label: Battle of Legnica
events.description: A Mongol diversionary force defeated a combined Polish and Teutonic Knights army in Silesia, demonstrating the scope of the European invasion.

events.label: Batu Khan
events.description: Grandson of Genghis Khan who led the devastating invasion of Europe and established the Golden Horde, ruling the western Mongol domains.

events.label: Sack of Kyiv
events.description: Batu Khan's forces destroyed Kyiv, one of the greatest cities of medieval Europe, effectively ending Kievan Rus' as a political entity.

events.label: Berke Khan converts to Islam
events.description: Golden Horde ruler Berke became the first Mongol khan to convert to Islam, allying with the Mamluks against his cousin Hulegu's Ilkhanate.

events.label: First invasion of Japan
events.description: Kublai Khan's first attempt to conquer Japan was repelled by Japanese defenders and a storm, foreshadowing the larger failed invasion of 1281.

events.label: John of Montecorvino in Beijing
events.description: Franciscan missionary who became the first Catholic Archbishop of Beijing, establishing a Christian community at the Yuan court.

events.label: Yelü Chucai advises Genghis
events.description: Khitan scholar Yelü Chucai entered Mongol service after the fall of Beijing and persuaded Genghis and Ögedei to tax rather than slaughter conquered populations, saving millions of lives.

events.label: Sorghaghtani Beki
events.description: Daughter-in-law of Genghis Khan, Sorghaghtani Beki managed the family appanages and positioned her four sons — Möngke, Kublai, Hülegü, and Ariq Böke — to rule the empire's successor states, making her arguably the most powerful woman in Mongol history.

events.label: Nestorian Christianity at Karakorum
events.description: Nestorian Christian churches operated openly in Karakorum alongside Buddhist temples and a mosque, reflecting the empire's official religious pluralism and the influence of Nestorian Kerait and Ongut tribes within the Mongol elite.

events.label: Pax Mongolica and the paiza
events.description: The Mongol Empire issued paiza tablets — metal passes guaranteeing safe passage, lodging, and horses — enabling merchants, diplomats, and missionaries to cross Eurasia with unprecedented security during the so-called Pax Mongolica.

events.label: Silver Tree Fountain of Karakorum
events.description: French silversmith Guillaume Boucher completed the Silver Tree for Ögedei Khan's palace at Karakorum — a monumental fountain with four spouts dispensing wine, mead, rice wine, and grape wine, powered by a bellows-driven angel at the crown.

events.label: Juvayni's History of the World Conqueror
events.description: Persian administrator Ata-Malik Juvayni completed his Tarikh-i Jahan-gusha (History of the World Conqueror), a firsthand account of Mongol rule written from within the Ilkhanate administration and an indispensable primary source.

events.label: Berke–Hülegü War
events.description: Golden Horde Khan Berke attacked his cousin Hülegü over the sack of Baghdad and unpaid tribute, the first open war between Mongol successor states and a sign of the empire's permanent fragmentation.

events.label: Genoese Colony at Caffa
events.description: The Golden Horde granted Genoa rights to the Black Sea port of Caffa (modern Feodosia), establishing a trading hub that funneled Chinese silks and Central Asian goods to European markets and exemplified the commercial dimension of the Pax Mongolica.

events.label: Tabriz as Ilkhanate Capital
events.description: Hülegü Khan made Tabriz the administrative capital of the Ilkhanate; under his successors it became one of the wealthiest cities in the world, a center of manuscript illumination and the point where Italian merchants first encountered Persian luxury goods.

events.label: Chabi — Kublai's Chief Consort
events.description: Chabi, Kublai Khan's favorite wife, was a skilled political operator who advocated leniency toward conquered Chinese subjects, fostered Tibetan Buddhist influence at court, and personally designed new quiver and helmet styles for the Yuan army.

events.label: Yuan Grand Canal extension
events.description: Kublai Khan's engineers extended and re-routed the Grand Canal to run directly from Hangzhou to Dadu (Beijing), creating a 1,100-mile inland waterway that supplied the new capital with grain from the south.

events.label: Mongol invasion of Java
events.description: Kublai Khan sent a fleet to punish the Javanese kingdom of Singhasari; the expedition was manipulated by local prince Raden Wijaya into defeating a rival ruler, after which Wijaya turned on the Mongols and drove them from the island — a humiliating final failure of Yuan overseas expansion.

events.label: Battle of Wadi al-Khaznadar
events.description: Ilkhan Ghazan's forces defeated the Mamluks at Wadi al-Khaznadar (the third Battle of Homs), briefly occupying Damascus and showing that Mongol military power in the Middle East was not yet spent after Ain Jalut.

events.label: Ibn Battuta in the Golden Horde
events.description: Moroccan traveler Ibn Battuta visited the Golden Horde court of Özbeg Khan and crossed the Chagatai Khanate on his way to India, leaving the most detailed eyewitness account of Mongol successor-state society in the late Pax Mongolica era.

events.label: Yesügei poisoned by Tatars
events.description: Yesügei, chieftain of the Borjigin clan and father of Temüjin, was poisoned by Tatar enemies while returning from delivering his son to a prospective bride. His death left the nine-year-old Temüjin and his family abandoned by their clan and destitute on the steppe — the trauma that forged Genghis Khan's iron will.

events.label: Temüjin's wife Börte kidnapped
events.description: Rival Merkit tribesmen raided Temüjin's camp and abducted his new bride Börte. Temüjin's subsequent rescue campaign — with help from his sworn brother Jamukha and the powerful Kereit ruler Toghrul — was his first act of military leadership and demonstrated the coalition-building that would define his rise.

events.label: Alliance with Toghrul (Wang Khan)
events.description: Temüjin forged a pivotal alliance with Toghrul, ruler of the powerful Kereit tribe and vassal of the Jin dynasty who held the honorific title Wang Khan. The partnership gave Temüjin legitimacy and military muscle; together they crushed the Tatars, Temüjin's father's killers, around 1202.

events.label: Defeat of Wang Khan
events.description: Temüjin defeated and killed Toghrul (Wang Khan) after their alliance broke down, absorbing the Kereit people into his growing confederation. This eliminated the last major rival power in Mongolia east of the Altai and left only the Naimans standing between Temüjin and total steppe supremacy.

events.label: Defeat of the Naimans
events.description: Temüjin crushed the Naiman confederation under Tayang Khan at the Battle of Chakirmaut, completing his unification of the Mongolian plateau. The Naiman scribes, who used the Uyghur script, were absorbed into Mongol service — which is how the Mongols acquired their first writing system.

events.label: Kurultai of 1206
events.description: At a grand assembly (kurultai) on the banks of the Onon River, all Mongol tribal leaders gathered to formally acclaim Temüjin as Genghis Khan — meaning 'universal ruler' or 'oceanic king.' The kurultai established the new state's laws, military decimal system (arban-zuun-mingan-tumen), and the Nine White Banners as the empire's symbol.

events.label: First campaign against Western Xia
events.description: Genghis Khan launched his first major campaign beyond the Mongolian plateau, invading the Tangut-ruled Western Xia kingdom. Western Xia submitted and paid tribute, becoming a vassal but not yet conquered — the campaign was partly a rehearsal for the coming war against the Jin dynasty.

events.label: Capture of Zhongdu (Beijing)
events.description: After four years of campaigning through northern China, Mongol forces captured and sacked Zhongdu, the Jin capital (modern Beijing), killing much of its population. Yelü Chucai, a Khitan official serving the Jin, was captured here and entered Mongol service, eventually moderating their approach to governing settled peoples.

events.label: Sack of Bukhara
events.description: Genghis Khan led his army in a surprise flanking march through the supposedly impassable Kyzylkum Desert to take Bukhara from behind. In the city's great mosque he reportedly declared himself the flail of God punishing the people for their sins. The city's inhabitants were spared; its garrison was massacred.

events.label: Sack of Urgench
events.description: Urgench, the Khwarezmian capital and one of the largest cities in the world, was besieged and sacked after fierce street-by-street fighting. The Mongols diverted the Amu Darya river through the ruined city. Contemporary estimates suggest over a million inhabitants were killed, though modern historians consider such figures exaggerated.

events.label: Battle of the Indus
events.description: Genghis Khan cornered Jalal ad-Din Mingburnu, the last Khwarezmian prince, at the Indus River after a chase across Afghanistan. Jalal ad-Din made a desperate stand with 30,000 men; after defeat he leapt his horse off a cliff into the river and swam to the Indian bank as the Mongols watched in astonishment. The escape ended the Khwarezmian dynasty.

events.label: John of Plano Carpini's mission
events.description: Pope Innocent IV sent Franciscan friar Giovanni da Pian del Carpine (John of Plano Carpini) as the first papal envoy to the Mongol Great Khan. Carpini attended Güyük Khan's coronation and returned in 1247 with a detailed report — Historia Mongalorum — the first systematic European account of Mongol customs, military tactics, and society.

events.label: Tolui's regency
events.description: After Genghis Khan's death, his youngest son Tolui served as regent for two years per Mongol custom — the youngest son (otchigin) kept the family hearth. Tolui commanded the forces that completed the destruction of the Jin heartland before his brother Ögedei was elected Great Khan at the kurultai of 1229.

events.label: Karakorum city walls and palace built
events.description: Ögedei Khan directed the construction of permanent walled city at Karakorum with a great palace (Tumen Amugulang), Chinese Buddhist temples, Muslim mosques, a Nestorian church, and the famous Silver Tree fountain. It was the administrative nerve center of an empire spanning half the world.

events.label: Töregene Khatun's regency
events.description: After Ögedei Khan's sudden death, his widow Töregene Khatun governed the Mongol Empire as regent for five years, maneuvering skillfully to ensure the election of her son Güyük as Great Khan. She dismissed Ögedei's chief ministers and installed her own favorites — Fatima, a Persian woman, wielding enormous influence through her.

events.label: Güyük Khan
events.description: Ögedei's son Güyük was elected Great Khan at a great kurultai attended by dignitaries from across Eurasia, including the papal envoy John of Plano Carpini. Güyük was hostile to his cousin Batu Khan of the Golden Horde and was reportedly marching to war against him when he died suddenly in 1248, possibly from alcohol-related illness.

events.label: Final Western Xia campaign
events.description: Genghis Khan personally led the final punitive campaign against Western Xia, which had refused to contribute troops for the Khwarezmian invasion. The campaign was successful but Genghis Khan died during the siege — of illness, according to most sources, though the exact cause remains debated. His generals kept his death secret until Western Xia surrendered.

events.label: Berke Khan converts to Islam
events.description: Berke, younger brother of Batu Khan and future ruler of the Golden Horde, converted to Islam during a visit to Bukhara — becoming the first member of the Genghisid royal house to convert. His conversion predated his rule but shaped his later alliance with the Mamluk Sultanate against the Ilkhanate.

events.label: Sack of Alamut — Assassins destroyed
events.description: Hülegü Khan captured and demolished the Nizari Ismaili fortress of Alamut in the Alborz Mountains, ending the feared Order of Assassins as a military power. The great library at Alamut — said to contain irreplaceable scientific manuscripts — was destroyed along with the fortress, though Nasir al-Din al-Tusi was rescued from captivity here and entered Mongol service.

events.label: Nasir al-Din al-Tusi at Maragheh
events.description: Captured from Alamut, the brilliant Persian mathematician and astronomer Nasir al-Din al-Tusi became Hülegü's court scientist and designed the Maragheh Observatory. There he developed the 'Tusi couple' — a mathematical device using two circles to model planetary motion without the problematic Ptolemaic equant, a breakthrough later used by Copernicus.

events.label: Buddhist–Taoist Debate at Karakorum
events.description: Möngke Khan presided over a grand theological debate at Karakorum between Buddhist and Taoist representatives, with Nestorian Christians and Muslims in attendance. The Buddhists were declared winners, but the event illustrated the remarkable intellectual diversity of the Mongol court at its peak — rulers who let all religions compete on merit.

events.label: Battle of Köse Dağ
events.description: A Mongol force under Baiju decisively defeated the Seljuk Sultanate of Rum at Köse Dağ in Anatolia, reducing the once-powerful sultanate to a Mongol vassal state. The battle opened Anatolia to Mongol influence and set the stage for the eventual displacement that would produce the Ottoman state.

events.label: Sack of Vladimir
events.description: Batu Khan's forces captured and burned Vladimir, the capital of the Vladimir-Suzdal principality and the most powerful city in northeastern Rus'. Grand Prince Yuri II fled but was killed at the Battle of the Sit River soon after. The destruction of Vladimir effectively ended the Kievan Rus' political order in the northeast.

events.label: Öz Beg makes Islam state religion of Golden Horde
events.description: Öz Beg Khan's accession to the Golden Horde throne marked a decisive Islamization of the western successor state; he made Islam the official state religion, forced the conversion of Mongol nobles, and built mosques throughout his territory. His long reign (1313–1341) was the Golden Horde's political and commercial peak.

events.label: Siege of Caffa and the Black Death
events.description: Golden Horde Khan Janibeg besieged the Genoese trading colony of Caffa in Crimea and, according to the chronicler Gabriele de' Mussis, catapulted plague-infected corpses over the walls — an early instance of biological warfare. Fleeing Genoese sailors carried the Black Death to Sicily and then across Europe.

events.label: Siege of Xiangyang
events.description: Kublai Khan's forces besieged the twin fortress-cities of Xiangyang and Fandeng on the Han River for five years — the longest siege in the Mongol-Song war. Only the arrival of giant counterweight trebuchets (manjaniqs) from Persian engineers broke the deadlock. The fall opened the Yangtze valley to the final Song conquest.

events.label: Battle of Yamen — proto-firearms
events.description: The final naval battle that destroyed the Southern Song fleet at Yamen saw Yuan forces employ fire-lance weapons — tube-launched projectiles that are among the earliest documented proto-firearms. Over 100,000 Song loyalists reportedly drowned or died in the battle; the last child emperor was carried into the sea by a minister who refused to surrender.

events.label: Arghun's diplomatic overtures to Europe
events.description: Ilkhan Arghun sent multiple embassies to European monarchs and the Pope proposing a Franco-Mongol alliance against the Mamluk Sultanate — the most ambitious attempt at East-West diplomatic coordination in the medieval era. His Nestorian-Christian envoy Rabban Sauma met Philip IV of France and Pope Nicholas IV in 1287–1288, but no military alliance materialized.

events.label: Tegüder Ahmad — first Muslim Ilkhan
events.description: Ilkhan Tegüder Ahmad, who had been baptized a Nestorian Christian, converted to Islam and took the name Ahmad, becoming the first Ilkhan to rule as a Muslim. His conversion alarmed Christian allies and Mongol traditionalists; he was overthrown and executed by his nephew Arghun after only two years, but his reign foreshadowed the eventual Islamization of the Ilkhanate.

events.label: Ghazan Khan's administrative reforms
events.description: Ilkhan Ghazan converted to Islam and simultaneously launched sweeping administrative reforms: standardizing weights and measures, reforming coinage, protecting farmers from arbitrary taxation by soldiers, and sponsoring an agricultural revival in war-ravaged Persia. His chief minister Rashid al-Din oversaw both the reforms and a renaissance of Persian arts and scholarship.

events.label: Death of Abu Sa'id — Ilkhanate fragments
events.description: Ilkhan Abu Sa'id died without an heir, triggering immediate succession conflicts. Within a decade the Ilkhanate had dissolved into a patchwork of competing Mongol, Persian, and Kurdish successor dynasties including the Jalayirids, Chobanids, and Muzaffarids. The collapse left Persia politically fragmented until Timur reunited it by conquest.

events.label: Kebek Khan's currency reform
events.description: Chagatai Khan Kebek introduced a standardized silver coin (the kebeki) that became the dominant currency across Central Asia for over a century. He also built a fixed palace at Qarshi — unusual for a khanate that had remained proudly nomadic — signaling a slow drift toward settled administration in the western Chagatai domains.

events.label: Tarmashirin converts Chagatai Khanate to Islam
events.description: Chagatai Khan Tarmashirin converted to Islam and began enforcing Islamic law, alienating the eastern Mongol tribes who remained shamanist or Buddhist. His assassination in 1334 split the Chagatai Khanate into the western Transoxiana (which became Muslim) and the eastern Moghulistan (which remained largely non-Muslim) — a fracture that persisted for a century.

events.label: Red Turban Rebellion
events.description: Inspired by White Lotus millenarian Buddhism, the Red Turban Rebellion erupted across the flooded Yellow River valley and spread into a general uprising against Yuan rule. From its ranks emerged Zhu Yuanzhang, a peasant orphan who would found the Ming dynasty, expel the Mongols, and reunite China — one of history's most improbable political ascents.

events.label: Bayan of the Baarin
events.description: Kublai Khan's most capable general, Bayan of the Baarin clan commanded the final Yuan campaign against the Southern Song, capturing hundreds of cities over five years and accepting the surrender of the child emperor in 1276. William of Rubruck had earlier met his Nestorian father at Karakorum. Bayan later served as senior minister under Kublai's successors.

events.label: Battle of Homs (1281)
events.description: Ilkhan Abaqa's forces, including Armenian and Georgian crusader allies, were defeated by the Mamluks at the Second Battle of Homs in Syria. The defeat confirmed that the Mongols could not break the Mamluk hold on the Levant and signaled that the Ilkhanate's westward expansion had permanently stalled.

events.label: Yuan drama (zaju) flourishes
events.description: Under Yuan rule, Chinese scholars blocked from the traditional civil service examination by Mongol preference for non-Chinese administrators turned to writing plays. The result was a golden age of Chinese drama: the zaju form, combining music, dialogue, and acrobatics, produced classics like The Injustice to Dou E by Guan Hanqing — works still performed today.

events.label: Toghon Temür flees to Mongolia
events.description: The last Yuan emperor, Toghon Temür, fled Khanbaliq (Beijing) as the Ming rebel army approached, retreating north to Shangdu (Xanadu). He died in 1370. The flight did not end Mongol political ambitions — his descendants maintained the 'Northern Yuan' on the steppe for another century — but it ended the Mongol dynasty's rule over China proper.

events.label: Hoelun — mother of Genghis Khan
events.description: After Yesügei's poisoning left the family destitute and abandoned by their clan, Hoelun kept Temüjin and his siblings alive by foraging roots, fishing, and shaming her sons into hunting small game. Her survival instincts and iron resolve during those starving years on the Onon River are the formative context for Genghis Khan's character. The Secret History treats her as the moral anchor of the foundational story.

events.label: Jamukha — sworn brother and rival
events.description: Jamukha was Temüjin's anda (sworn brother) who helped rescue Börte from the Merkits in 1184 and led a rival Mongol confederation for two decades. Charismatic, equally skilled in coalition-building, he was the last serious Mongol rival to Genghis Khan. Captured in 1205 after allies surrendered him, Jamukha refused mercy and was executed in the honorable bloodless manner — smothered or back-broken — per Temüjin's own order.

events.label: Mukhali administers northern China
events.description: Genghis Khan appointed Mukhali, one of his four senior generals and the son of a low-ranking vassal, as viceroy of the Jin campaigns while Genghis himself turned west toward Khwarezm. Mukhali commanded the continuing conquest of northern China from 1217 until his death in 1223, integrating Chinese and Khitan auxiliaries into the Mongol force — a key test of the merit-promotion system Genghis used to build his empire.

events.label: Inalchuq massacres Mongol trade caravan
events.description: The governor of Otrar on the Syr Darya river, Inalchuq (also called Ghayir Khan), declared a Mongol trade delegation of five hundred merchants to be spies and had them all executed, confiscating their goods. Genghis Khan sent envoys to Shah Muhammad II demanding Inalchuq's punishment; the Shah killed one envoy and sent the others home humiliated. The incident triggered the Mongol invasion of the Khwarezmian Empire in 1219.

events.label: Conquest of the Kara-Khitai
events.description: Genghis Khan sent his general Jebe to overthrow the Buddhist Khitan-led Kara-Khitai state in modern Xinjiang and eastern Central Asia — a remnant of the Liao dynasty that had been displaced by the Jin around 1125. The Kara-Khitai had harbored the Naiman leader Kuchlug, who had seized their throne. Jebe conquered the region in a single campaign, extending Mongol power to the borders of Khwarezm and making the western invasion of 1219 possible.

events.label: Destruction of the House of Wisdom
events.description: When Hülegü Khan sacked Baghdad, Mongol soldiers threw the contents of the Bayt al-Hikma — Baghdad's great manuscript library — into the Tigris River. Eyewitnesses described the river running black with ink for days. The House of Wisdom had functioned since the eighth century as a translation academy and manuscript repository containing Greek, Persian, Indian, and Syriac texts, some of which existed nowhere else. The loss is one of the largest single destructions of accumulated knowledge in pre-modern history.

events.label: Kitbuqa killed at Ayn Jalut
events.description: Kitbuqa, a Naiman Nestorian Christian and one of Hülegü's most senior generals, was left in command of the Mongol occupation force in Syria when Hülegü withdrew east after Möngke Khan's death. At the Battle of Ayn Jalut in September 1260, the Mamluk general Baybars used a feigned retreat to draw Kitbuqa's army into a prepared killing zone. Kitbuqa was captured and executed — the first Mongol field commander to be defeated and killed in a pitched battle.

events.label: Ariq Böke's rival kurultai
events.description: Ariq Böke, the youngest of Tolui's sons and regent at Karakorum, called his own kurultai to claim the title of Great Khan in 1260, directly contesting Kublai's parallel election at Kaiping. Ariq Böke represented the steppe traditionalist faction who believed the empire should remain anchored in Mongolia. Kublai cut off Karakorum's grain supply and starved him into surrender by 1264. Ariq Böke died in custody in 1266, possibly poisoned, ending the debate about whether the Mongol Empire would be a steppe polity or a Chinese-style dynasty.

events.label: Sarai — Golden Horde capital
events.description: Batu Khan established Sarai (also called Old Sarai or Sarai Batu) on the lower Volga near modern Astrakhan as the administrative capital of the Golden Horde. The city grew into one of the largest in the medieval world, with a population estimated at one hundred thousand at its peak, containing mosques, churches, craft workshops, and a resident Genoese merchant quarter. All Russian princes were required to travel to Sarai to receive their yarlyk (patent of rule) from the khan.

events.label: Lu Xiufu's last stand at Yamen
events.description: As the Yuan fleet destroyed the last Song loyalist fleet at Yamen in March 1279, the senior Song minister Lu Xiufu made the decision that the child emperor Zhao Bing should not survive to become a Mongol captive. He took the seven-year-old emperor in his arms and walked into the sea. Over a hundred thousand Song loyalists reportedly died in the battle or drowned themselves rather than surrender. It is one of the most dramatic endings of any dynasty in Chinese history.
